title: Consumer-Driven Contract Testing
description: Verify provider/consumer contracts; reduces integration surprises; supports independent deployability.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Contract testing is primarily a testing methodology with minimal infrastructure costs. Min=$0 represents small teams running contract tests in existing CI/CD pipelines with no additional infrastructure. Max=$500 accounts for: (1) Pact Broker hosting on cloud (t3.small EC2 ~$15-30/month or managed service like PactFlow starting at $250-500/month for teams), (2) Additional CI/CD compute time for contract verification builds (~$50-100/month for frequent contract verification across multiple services), (3) Contract test environment costs if dedicated (~$100-200/month for lightweight test instances). Most teams using open-source Pact with self-hosted broker will be in the $50-150/month range.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity adoption. Factors: (1) Learning curve: Teams must understand contract testing philosophy, consumer-driven design, and tools like Pact (~40-60 hours training/research), (2) Initial setup: Configure Pact Broker, integrate with CI/CD pipelines, establish contract versioning strategy (~80-120 hours), (3) Refactoring: Retrofit existing services to support contract testing, write initial contracts for critical integrations (~120-160 hours), (4) Process changes: Establish workflows for contract changes, breaking changes, versioning policies (~40-60 hours), (5) Team coordination: Multiple teams (consumers/providers) must align on contract testing practices. Total estimated effort: 280-400 hours at blended rate of $75-100/hour = $2,100-4,000. The $2,500 mid-point reflects typical implementation with 2-4 services and 2-3 teams.
provenance.licenseCost: Open-source tools dominate this space. Pact (most popular contract testing framework) is Apache 2.0 licensed and free. Pact Broker is also open-source. Spring Cloud Contract is Apache 2.0. Commercial options exist (PactFlow managed service at $250-500/month, SmartBear services) but are subscription-based, not one-time licenses. Most organizations start with free open-source tools. Enterprise support contracts are optional ($5,000-20,000/year) but not required for adoption. Setting license cost to $0 for typical open-source adoption path.

provides.reasoning: This pattern specifically implements consumer-driven contract testing, which is a distinct testing capability that verifies service interactions through defined contracts.
The pattern verifies that providers honor their contracts with consumers, providing explicit validation that both sides of the contract are properly implemented.

Contract testing enables services to be deployed independently by ensuring compatibility without requiring full integration testing, though this is more of an enablement outcome than a direct capability.
Many contract testing tools generate stubs or mocks from contracts for consumer testing, allowing consumers to develop against provider contracts without the actual provider running.

requires.reasoning: Contract testing fundamentally requires defined contracts between consumers and providers, typically expressed in formats like Pact files, OpenAPI specs, or other contract definition languages.
Consumer-driven contract testing requires a testing framework to execute contract tests, such as Pact, Spring Cloud Contract, or similar tools that can run automated contract verification.

supports_nfr.reason: Contract testing supports GDPR-compliant and non-compliant systems as it verifies API contracts.
Contract testing supports HIPAA-compliant and non-compliant systems as it verifies API contracts.
Contract testing supports CCPA-compliant and non-compliant systems as it verifies API contracts.
Contract testing supports SOX-compliant and non-compliant systems as it verifies API contracts.

supports_constraints.reason: Contract testing is cloud-agnostic and works across all deployment environments.
Consumer-driven contract testing is a methodology that can be implemented across all programming languages.
Contract testing applies to any platform where services or components communicate via APIs.

contract_testing_tool.description: Contract testing framework. Pact is polyglot and widely adopted; Spring Cloud Contract integrates with Spring ecosystem; Postman for API-first teams; custom for bespoke solutions.

contract_storage.description: Where contracts are stored and shared. Broker (Pact Broker) enables dynamic verification and webhooks; Git for version control integration; artifact repository for existing CI/CD workflows.

verification_trigger.description: When provider verifies contracts. On provider change catches breaking changes early; on consumer change validates new requirements; both for maximum safety; scheduled for periodic validation.

consumer_version_selectors.description: Which consumer versions to verify against. Deployed/released ensures production compatibility; all versions for comprehensive testing; main branch for active development; tagged for specific releases.

breaking_change_policy.description: Action on contract violations. Fail build prevents merging breaking changes; warn only for gradual adoption; block deployment prevents production issues while allowing development.
